children.name: Malurus
children.description: The typical fairy-wrens — a genus of small, active insectivores famous for the brilliant breeding plumage of males, which features iridescent blues and purples. Endemic to Australia and New Guinea, they are socially monogamous but sexually promiscuous, with pair bonds frequently overridden by extra-pair copulations. Their cooperative breeding systems are among the most complex in the passerine world.

children.name: Malurus cyaneus
children.description: Australia's most familiar fairy-wren, the Superb Fairy-wren is common in gardens, parks, and woodlands across the southeast. The breeding male is unmistakable with its brilliant blue and black plumage, while females and eclipse males are a warm brown with a rufous eye-patch. It is a cooperative breeder with a notoriously promiscuous mating system.

children.name: Malurus splendens
children.description: A dazzling fairy-wren of arid and semi-arid Australia, the breeding male is a vivid turquoise-blue with a violet throat and a black breast band. It inhabits mallee, mulga, and dry scrublands, where it lives in small family groups. Like most fairy-wrens, it is a cooperative breeder and extremely active, foraging constantly in low shrubs and on the ground.

children.name: Malurus lamberti
children.description: A widespread fairy-wren with a breeding male in brilliant blue with a violet crown and black-and-blue wing coverts, found across much of mainland Australia. Females are a soft grey-brown with a pale eyebrow and a bluish tail. It occupies dry forests, mallee, and shrublands, often foraging in mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Malurus melanocephalus
children.description: A small, striking fairy-wren in which the breeding male is black with a brilliant red back and a blue-black tail, endemic to northern and eastern Australia. It favours grassy woodlands, swamps, and riparian edges, where it flicks its tail constantly while foraging. This species is an opportunistic breeder that responds rapidly to rainfall.

children.name: Malurus leucopterus
children.description: An extraordinary fairy-wren exhibiting dramatic geographic variation in male plumage. On the mainland, the breeding male is cobalt blue with white wings, but on Dirk Hartog Island the male is entirely black-and-white and on Barrow Island it is a deep violet-blue with white wings. Females everywhere are a soft mouse-brown with a pale blue tail.

children.name: Malurus amabilis
children.description: A striking fairy-wren with bright blue upperparts in the breeding male, a black face mask, and a blue crown, found in lowland forests of northern Australia and southern New Guinea. It favours rainforest edges and dense thickets near water. Its lovely, high-pitched song is a characteristic sound of Cape York forests.

children.name: Malurus assimilis
children.description: A recently split species from the Variegated Fairy-wren complex, with the breeding male showing a purple-blue back and crown, found across inland and western Australia. It inhabits dry shrublands, mulga, and saltbush plains. Females are a soft grey-brown with a pale blue tail.

children.name: Malurus pulcherrimus
children.description: A beautiful fairy-wren with the breeding male showing a brilliant blue breast and crown, a black face mask, and blue-black upperparts, restricted to the mallee and heathlands of southern Australia. It is a cooperative breeder, living in small family groups.

children.name: Malurus coronatus
children.description: A distinctive fairy-wren with a purple crown in the breeding male, chestnut ear coverts, and a long blue tail, found in dense riparian vegetation along rivers and creeks in northern Australia. Its population is threatened by habitat degradation from cattle grazing and altered fire regimes.

children.name: Malurus grayi
children.description: A large fairy-wren with a distinctive broad, flattened bill, blue upperparts in the male, and a long tail, found in lowland and montane forests of New Guinea. It forages actively in the understorey, often in mixed-species flocks. Its broad bill is an adaptation for gleaning insects from a wide surface area.

children.name: Malurus campbelli
children.description: A little-known fairy-wren endemic to the forests of New Guinea, with blue upperparts in the male and a relatively short tail. It inhabits lowland and hill forests, foraging in the understorey. Its ecology remains poorly understood due to its remote distribution.

children.name: Malurus cyanocephalus
children.description: A spectacular fairy-wren with the male in striking blue-black plumage with a brilliant blue crown, found in lowland forests of New Guinea. It is the largest species of Malurus and one of the most vividly coloured. It forages actively in the understorey, often in family groups.

children.name: Malurus albomiculatus
children.description: A fairy-wren with white shoulder patch from northern New Guinea.

children.name: Malurus dorsalis
children.description: A fairy-wren with broad bill from New Guinea.

children.name: Malurus nigrifrons
children.description: A fairy-wren with black face from New Guinea.

children.name: Malurus viridinotus
children.description: A fairy-wren with green back from New Guinea.

children.name: Malurus kouslandii
children.description: A fairy-wren from the lowland forests of New Guinea.

children.name: Clytomyias
children.description: A monotypic genus of fairy-wren containing the Orange-crowned Fairy-wren, endemic to the montane forests of New Guinea. It has a distinctive orange crown and a relatively long, slender bill, differing from Malurus in both plumage and behaviour. It is a shy, secretive inhabitant of the forest understorey.

children.name: Clytomyias insignis
children.description: A small, secretive fairy-wren with a bright orange crown, olive-brown upperparts, and pale underparts, endemic to the montane forests of New Guinea above 2,000 metres. It forages in the dense understorey, often in mixed-species flocks. Its thin, high-pitched calls are a characteristic sound of the New Guinea highlands.

children.name: Stipiturus
children.description: The emu-wrens — a genus of tiny, secretive fairy-wrens characterised by greatly elongated, filamentous tail feathers consisting of only six feathers, resembling the plumage of the emu. They are restricted to Australia, inhabiting heathlands, swamps, and sedgelands. Their weak flight and skulking behaviour make them notoriously difficult to observe.

children.name: Stipiturus malachurus
children.description: A tiny, secretive bird with an extraordinary wispy tail that accounts for half its total length, found in coastal heaths and wetlands across southern Australia. Its fawn-and-brown streaked plumage provides perfect camouflage in dense sedges and button-grass. Southern Emu-wrens are more often heard than seen, their thin, high-pitched calls revealing their presence in the undergrowth.

children.name: Stipiturus ruficeps
children.description: A tiny emu-wren with a rufous crown, finely streaked brown upperparts, and the characteristic wispy tail of the genus, found in spinifex grasslands of arid and semi-arid Australia. It is a secretive, skulking bird that is more often heard than seen, its thin, high-pitched calls revealing its presence in the hummock grass.

children.name: Stipiturus mallee
children.description: A tiny, endangered emu-wren restricted to a small area of mallee shrubland in north-western Victoria and adjacent South Australia. Its plumage is finely streaked brown and black with a pale eyebrow. It is one of Australia's rarest passerines, with a population of fewer than 10,000 individuals threatened by wildfire and habitat fragmentation.

children.name: Stipiturus roserius
children.description: An emu-wren from the highlands of New Guinea.

children.name: Amytornis
children.description: The grasswrens — a genus of secretive, ground-dwelling birds endemic to Australia, with finely streaked plumage providing excellent camouflage in the spinifex and tussock grasslands they inhabit. They have strong legs and relatively long tails, and are notoriously difficult to observe due to their skulking behaviour. Their cryptic plumage and weak flight make them among Australia's most challenging birds to study.

children.name: Amytornis textilis
children.description: A medium-sized grasswren with finely streaked grey-brown upperparts, a white throat, and a long, graduated tail, found in shrublands and saltbush plains of western and southern Australia. It skulks through dense low vegetation, foraging for insects on the ground. Its loud, chattering calls betray its presence in the arid bush.

children.name: Amytornis merrotsyi
children.description: A small, short-tailed grasswren with finely streaked brown upperparts and a white throat, restricted to rocky slopes and spinifex-covered hills in the Flinders Ranges and Gawler Ranges of South Australia. It is a secretive, ground-dwelling bird that darts between rock crevices when disturbed.

children.name: Amytornis barbatus
children.description: A pale grey-brown grasswren with fine streaking, a white throat, and a long tail, found in the dense lignum swamps and canegrass of the Bulloo River basin in south-western Queensland and adjacent New South Wales. It is a shy, elusive bird that is extremely difficult to observe in its impenetrable wetland habitat.

children.name: Amytornis housei
children.description: A striking, dark grasswren with sooty-black plumage, a white belly and vent, and a relatively short tail, restricted to sandstone escarpments and spinifex-covered slopes in the Kimberley region of Western Australia. It is a shy, elusive bird that bounds across rocky terrain with remarkable agility.

children.name: Amytornis dorotheae
children.description: A grasswren from the Gulf Country of northern Australia, considered near-threatened.

children.name: Amytornis striatus
children.description: A grasswren with fine striations from mallee and heathlands of southern Australia.

children.name: Amytornis goyderi
children.description: A grasswren from the Simpson Desert region.
